title: Roughening of active nonlinear interfaces with broken tilt symmetry
authors: Ailén M. Cámara, Alejandro B. Kolton, José Luis Iguaín
abstract: We study the roughening of an interface with nonlinear elasticity driven by temporally correlated noise, which breaks statistical tilt symmetry. Using scaling arguments and a self-consistent Hartree approximation, we derive the crossover diagram and the steady-state structure factor. We identify three scaling regimes associated with the Larkin, anharmonic Larkin, and Edwards--Wilkinson universality classes, and obtain the crossover lengths separating them. Numerical simulations of large systems confirm the analytical predictions over the full parameter range. Our results provide a unified description of finite-size and crossover effects in a minimal nonlinear-elastic Ornstein--Uhlenbeck active interface.
summary: This work theoretically analyzes how nonlinear interfaces roughen when subjected to colored active noise that breaks symmetry. It successfully derives a crossover diagram showing the transition between three distinct physical regimes: Larkin, anharmonic Larkin, and Edwards–Wilkinson scaling. The findings provide a unified framework for understanding finite-size effects in such non-equilibrium systems.
main_problem: The study focuses on characterizing the roughening dynamics of active nonlinear interfaces driven by temporally correlated noise that breaks statistical tilt symmetry.
main_result: The system exhibits a unified description across three distinct self-affine scaling regimes: Larkin (LM), anharmonic Larkin (ALM), and Edwards–Wilkinson (EW).
method: The analysis employs scaling arguments, the self-consistent Hartree approximation, and comparison with known asymptotic limits.
model_or_system: The model describes an overdamped interface height h(x, t) governed by a nonlinear elastic energy functional and driven by an Ornstein–Uhlenbeck colored noise process. The dynamics are captured by a nonlinear partial differential equation.
key_observables: Structure factor $\langle|h_q|^2angle = S(q)$, roughness exponent ($\zeta$), and dynamical exponent ($z$).
important_parameters: The crossover diagram depends on the normalized length scale $	ilde{\ell}$ and a dimensionless coupling constant $\Gamma_{2n}$, which controls the transition between regimes.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations like scaling arguments and the Hartree approximation to derive analytical boundaries for transitions between universality classes.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the crossover diagram mapping the EW, LM, and ALM regimes using dimensionless parameters, with data collapsing onto universal master curves in numerical simulations.
paper_structure: The paper develops the governing equation, analyzes the linear limit (LM to EW crossover), extends the analysis to include nonlinearity via the Hartree approximation, and finally presents a unified crossover diagram showing the interplay between all three regimes.
why_interesting: While focused on classical interface dynamics, the concept of universality classes, non-equilibrium steady states, and crossovers driven by noise correlations are fundamental concepts applicable to many open quantum systems and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics problems in AMO physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Crossover diagram for the non-linear active inter- face described by Eq. (1), mapped as a function of the normal- ized length scale ˜ℓ= ℓ/√c2τ and the dimensionless coupling constant Γ2n = c2nT/(c(3n−1)/2 2 √τ). Here, T denotes the ac- tive noise strength, τ is the temporal correlation time, c2 is the linear elastic constant, and c2n (with n > 1) represents the degree of non-Hookean elasticity. The analytical bound- aries mapping the transitions between the EW, LM, and ALM roughening regimes are defined by Eqs. (8)–(10).

Figure 2. Analytical predictions for the Larkin (LM) to Edwards-Wilkinson (EW) crossover during the relaxation of a flat initial interface, for τ = 10. (a) S(q, t) vs q at dif- ferent times. The dotted line shows EW scaling ∼q−2 and the dashed-dotted the LM scaling S(q, t) ∼q−(1+2ζLM) = q−4. (b) S(q, t) vs t for q > q∗and q < q∗, with q∗

Figure 3. Γ4 < 2. Top: Structure factors for several set of parameters (in the key). Bottom: The same data collapsed onto a master curve. The line represents the analytical solu- tion g<(k, Γ4)/k2. Dashed (blue) part and dash-dotted (red) parts correspond EW and LM regimes, respectively.

Figure 4. Structure factors for Γ4 = 8. The plots correspond to c2 = 1, c4 = 8 and several pairs of T (in the key) and τ, which satisfy T/√τ = 1. Top panel: raw data. Bottom panel: rescaled data collapsing on an universal function. The line represents the analytical solution g>(k, Γ4)/k2. Dashed (blue), solid (dark-green) and dash-dotted (red) parts corre- spond to EW. ALM and LM regimes, respectively

Figure 5. Structure factor for Γ4 > 2. The plots correspond to four different Γ4 (in the key) and pairs of T (in the key) and τ, which satisfy T/√τ = 1. Data points are the results of numerical simulations and the lines represent the analytical solutions: dashed (blue), solid (dark-green) and dash-dotted (red) parts belong to EW. ALM and LM regimes, respectively. In all panels the structure factor is presented in a rotated manner (see eq. (73)) to better appreciate the widening of the ALM zone with increasing Γ4.
